message:
perf(btree): specialize CellRef::parse for LeafTable pages

CellRef::parse handled all four page kinds (interior table, leaf
table, interior index, leaf index) through a single generic body that
branched repeatedly on page_type. LeafTable cells dominate the
aggregate scan hot path (table b-trees with no overflow), so the
generic path runs millions of times in a typical SUM/COUNT scan
across a large table.

Add an inlined fast path: if page_type is LeafTable, dispatch to a
new private parse_leaf_table() helper. The helper walks the cell
shape directly without the page_type matching the generic body
performs:

- read payload-size varint (with overflow-safe try_from u32 + offset
  arithmetic)
- read rowid varint
- compute local_size: max_local = usable_size - 35; if payload fits
  locally use it directly, otherwise call local_payload_size() for
  overflow-aware sizing
- bounds-check the local end against page.len() and usable_size, with
  DatabaseCorrupt errors that name the specific overflow surface
  ("payload bytes" vs "overflow pointer") so corrupt-page diagnostics
  stay precise
- read 4-byte big-endian overflow page pointer when local_size <
  payload_size; surface DatabaseCorrupt for a zero pointer via
  PageNumber::new(...).ok_or_else(...)

Returns the same CellRef shape the generic path produces:
left_child = None, rowid = Some(rowid), payload_size, local_size,
payload_offset, overflow_page. Existing callers see no behavior
change — only a faster parse for the LeafTable case.

The generic CellRef::parse body is unchanged for the other three
page types and is still the canonical implementation; the fast path
only short-circuits the LeafTable arm.
